text: Cognitive offloading is the practice of externalizing mental content — lists, schedules, ideas — onto the environment to free working memory for deeper processing. David Allen's Getting Things Done system is essentially a cognitive offloading architecture: by capturing every commitment in a trusted external system, the mind is relieved of the duty to remember and can instead focus. Neuroscientist Russ Poldrack's research shows that the prefrontal cortex, which manages goal-directed attention, is easily saturated. When it must simultaneously track unresolved items and perform analytical work, performance on both degrades. Offloading is not laziness — it is the deliberate reallocation of scarce neural resources.
question: According to the passage, what does Russ Poldrack's research reveal about the prefrontal cortex?

options.text: It performs best when multitasking between memory retrieval and analysis
It is easily saturated, and tracking unresolved items while doing analytical work degrades both
It can be trained to ignore distractions through repeated offloading exercises
It only activates when tasks are captured in an external system
